mnemonics: Think of a linear mapping as a machine that preserves vector addition and scalar multiplication: 'Add then map' gives the same result as 'Map then add'. The same goes for scaling: 'Scale then map' equals 'Map then scale'.
keyPoints: A linear mapping T: V -> W must satisfy T(u + v) = T(u) + T(v) and T(cv) = cT(v) for all vectors u, v and all scalars c.
The kernel (or null space) of T is the set of all vectors in V that map to the zero vector in W. It is a subspace of V.
The image (or range) of T is the set of all vectors in W that are the image of some vector in V. It is a subspace of W.
A linear mapping is one-to-one (injective) if and only if its kernel contains only the zero vector.
The rank-nullity theorem states: dim(V) = rank(T) + nullity(T), where rank is the dimension of the image and nullity is the dimension of the kernel.
commonMistakes: Assuming all functions between vector spaces are linear. A function must specifically satisfy the two linearity properties; it is not enough for it to 'look' straight.
Confusing the kernel with the image. The kernel is about what gets mapped *to* zero; the image is about what gets mapped *from* something.
Forgetting to check both properties (additivity and homogeneity) when proving a map is linear. Proving one does not prove the other.
